mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned CLK engine typically costs £1,800–£3,500 supply only depending on engine variant (M111, M112 or M272). A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 depending on your car model and engine type. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine your CLK has. The M112 V6 in the CLK 320 is regarded as a solid, reliable engine when properly maintained – it's the pick of the bunch. The M111 in the CLK 230 Kompressor is characterful but has timing chain and oil leak vulnerabilities. The M272 in the CLK 280 is a more modern engine but suffers from a well‑documented balance shaft gear failure on early engines (serial numbers below 468993).

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the CLK 230 (M111), the most common issues are timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster wear, and oil leaks from the camshaft actuator into the wiring loom. On the CLK 320 (M112), the main issue is valve cover oil leaks. On the CLK 280 (M272), the defining weakness is balance shaft gear failure (early engines) and swirl flap issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a CLK engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £3,000–£8,000 CLK, a £1,800–£3,500 recon is 20‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the M272 balance shaft has failed catastrophically, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing. The CLK is becoming a modern classic, so well‑preserved examples may justify the investment.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my CLK's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The CLK 230 Kompressor (1997‑2002) uses the M111.975 (193 PS) or M111.982 (197 PS). The CLK 320 (1998‑2010) uses the M112.940/.955. The CLK 280 (2005‑2010) uses the M272.940. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. For the M272, check the engine serial number – if it's above 468993, the balance shaft issue should be resolved.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a CLK engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – M111 timing chain wear (if caught early), M112 valve cover gaskets, M272 balance shaft wear (if caught early) – are repairable. However, if the M272 balance shaft has failed or the timing chain has snapped, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page must match. Sections 2, 4, and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: Not available — all engines are rebuilt or used

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the CLK pricing across generations. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 depending on the car model and engine type. The M272 (CLK 280) is at the upper end of the range due to its complexity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLK engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The CLK's engine bay provides reasonable access, though the V6 engines are more tightly packaged than the four‑cylinder.

problems.title: M111 Timing Chain Stretch & Camshaft Adjuster Wear (CLK 230 Kompressor)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noises on start‑up, power loss, rough running, engine backfires, metallic ticking from the engine, reduced acceleration. A clicking noise is fairly common on many M111s and is often caused by a faulty camshaft adjuster.
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£2,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps or jumps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on start‑up or ticking, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The M111's timing chain can stretch over time, and the camshaft adjuster can wear out. A clicking noise is fairly common on many M111s and is often caused by a faulty camshaft adjuster. The chain typically requires replacement after 160,000‑200,000 km. Using the incorrect engine oil grade is a major cause of timing chain issues. At the first sign of rattling or ticking, have the timing system inspected immediately. A £800–£2,000 preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a £1,800–£2,800 engine replacement.

problems.title: M111 Oil Leaks — Camshaft Actuator & Wiring Loom (CLK 230 Kompressor)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, engine warning lights, O2 sensor or MAF issues. Oil can leak from the camshaft variable timing actuator into the wiring loom.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 [PLACEHOLDER] (actuator seal replacement and wiring loom cleaning/replacement).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected or warning lights appear.

problems.recommendation: The M111 engine has issues with oil leaks from the camshaft variable timing actuator into the wiring loom. This eventually requires the loom to be cleaned or replaced and can cause O2 sensor or MAF issues. If you notice oil spots or warning lights, have the actuator seals and wiring harness inspected. Early intervention can prevent more expensive sensor failures.

problems.title: M112 Valve Cover Oil Leaks (CLK 320, 1998–2010)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the top of the engine. Oil leaks on the breathers and valve covers are very common on the M112.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: The M112 V6 is known for oil leaks from the valve cover gaskets. The breathers are sealed with RTV, but the valve covers use regular gaskets that can harden and leak over time. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the valve cover gaskets inspected. Replacement is relatively straightforward and much cheaper than letting the leak worsen. The M112 is regarded as a solid engine when properly maintained.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Gear Failure (CLK 280, 2005–2010)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light with codes P0016/P0017, rattling noises from the engine, loss of power, rough running, potential engine failure. The balance shaft gear is made of improperly hardened metal and can wear out.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (balance shaft and timing gear replacement — engine out job).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the engine warning light appears with P0016/P0017, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The M272 V6 is the defining weakness of the CLK 280. Early M272 engines (up to mid‑2006) suffer from balance shaft gear failure due to improperly hardened timing gears. This requires the engine to be removed for repair. Engines with serial numbers after 468993 are generally safe. Check your engine serial number before buying. If you see P0016/P0017 codes, have the balance shaft inspected immediately — ignoring it can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

problems.title: M272 Swirl Flap Issues (CLK 280, 2005–2010)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of low‑end torque, rough idle, poor fuel economy.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (swirl flap linkage repair or intake manifold replacement).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection soon if warning lights appear.

problems.recommendation: The M272 can suffer from swirl flap issues in the intake manifold. The swirl flaps can seize or the linkage can fail, causing engine warning lights and reduced performance. If you experience power loss or see warning lights, have the intake manifold inspected. Regular cleaning of the EGR system can help prevent this issue.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Catch it early — engine‑out job but cheaper than replacement
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* CLK 230 (M111): Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start
* CLK 320 (M112): Valve cover gaskets replaced and no oil leaks
* CLK 280 (M272): Engine serial number above 468993 — balance shaft issue resolved
* A well‑maintained example — the CLK is a modern classic in the making
avoidIf: * CLK 230 (M111): Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start or ticking
* CLK 230 (M111): Any sign of oil leaks into the wiring loom
* CLK 320 (M112): Any sign of oil leaks from the valve covers
* CLK 280 (M272): Any sign of rattling or P0016/P0017 codes — balance shaft warning
* CLK 280 (M272): Engine serial number below 468993 — high risk of balance shaft failure
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes CLK 230, 280 and 320 represent some of the most desirable petrol engines in the CLK range. The M112 V6 in the CLK 320 is regarded as a solid, reliable engine when properly maintained — it's the pick of the bunch for reliability. The M111 in the CLK 230 Kompressor offers characterful supercharged performance but has timing chain and oil leak vulnerabilities. The M272 in the CLK 280 is a more modern DOHC V6 but suffers from a well‑documented balance shaft gear failure on early engines. Buy on service history and engine serial number above all else — a well‑maintained CLK with the right engine is a rewarding and increasingly collectable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare CLK engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes CLK 230 / 280 / 320 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling? (M111 timing chain or M272 balance shaft warning)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Engine Serial Number — CLK 280 (M272): Check if above 468993 (balance shaft issue resolved)
4. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any P0016/P0017? (M272 balance shaft)
5. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve covers (M112) and wiring loom (M111)
6. Running Quality — Any rough idle, hesitation, or ticking? (camshaft adjuster or swirl flap warning)
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
